1:
"Thou therefore, my son, be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus." The brief verse notes the charge to be strong in the grace that is in Christ. The grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's tender, emphatic charge: "thou therefore, my son, be strong (endynamou — be strengthened, gain strength, be empowered) in the grace that is in Christ Jesus" — drawing the conclusion from his own example and the defections (ch.1), Paul charges Timothy to be strong.

On "be strong," the witnesses note that the strength is not self-derived. Barnes and Calvin note the passive form: "be strengthened" — Timothy is not to summon strength from himself but to be empowered by grace; the strength comes from God, received, not generated.

On "in the grace that is in Christ Jesus," the eastern Chrysostom notes the source: the strength is "in the grace that is in Christ Jesus" — the grace that flows from and resides in Christ; union with Christ is the believer's reservoir of strength. The verse displays the charge to be strong in the grace that is in Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's emphatic charge — "thou therefore, my son, be strong (be strengthened, empowered) in the grace that is in Christ Jesus," concluding from his example and the defections (Barnes, Robertson); the passive form, strength received from God not generated from self (Barnes, Calvin); "in the grace that is in Christ Jesus," union with Christ the reservoir of strength (Chrysostom). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the call: be strong. The verse displays the source: grace in Christ. The verse displays the passivity: be strengthened. The verse displays the address: my son. The charge displays Timothy strengthened by Christ's grace. The verse displays the strength: from grace. The verse displays the grace: in Christ. The verse displays the son: charged. The charge to be strong in the grace that is in Christ displays the keynote of the chapter: Paul tenderly charges his son Timothy to be strengthened — not by his own resources but by the grace that resides in Christ Jesus — for the faithful, suffering ministry to which he is called. The verse displays the call to strength, issuing in faithful transmission of the truth (v.2).
2:
"And the things that thou hast heard of me among many witnesses, the same commit thou to faithful men, who shall be able to teach others also." The witnesses note the charge to entrust the truth to faithful, teaching men. On "the things that thou hast heard of me among many witnesses," the grammatical voices note the deposit. Barnes and Robertson note the doctrine to be transmitted: "the things that thou hast heard of me among many witnesses" — the apostolic teaching Timothy received from Paul, attested publicly before many; the sound doctrine, openly delivered, not secret.

On "the same commit thou to faithful men," the witnesses note the transmission. Barnes and Calvin note the charge: "commit (paratheme — entrust, as a deposit) thou to faithful (trustworthy, reliable) men" — Timothy is to hand on the deposit to faithful men who will hold it true; the trust passes from generation to generation through reliable hands.

On "who shall be able to teach others also," the eastern Cyril notes the chain: the men chosen must be "able to teach others also" — not only faithful to hold the truth but competent to pass it on; the succession of sound teaching is secured through men both trustworthy and apt to teach. The verse displays the charge to entrust the truth to faithful, teaching men.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deposit — "the things that thou hast heard of me among many witnesses," the apostolic teaching publicly attested (Barnes, Robertson); "commit (entrust as a deposit) thou to faithful (trustworthy) men," handing on the trust to reliable hands (Barnes, Calvin); "who shall be able to teach others also," competent to pass it on, securing the succession (Cyril). The verse displays the transmission. The verse marks the deposit: heard of me. The verse displays the witnesses: many. The verse displays the trust: to faithful men. The verse displays the chain: able to teach others. The charge displays the truth handed on through faithful teachers. The verse displays the things: heard. The verse displays the men: faithful. The verse displays the teaching: of others. The charge to entrust the truth to faithful, teaching men displays Paul's care for the gospel's continuance: Timothy is to commit the apostolic teaching he received — publicly attested — to faithful men, themselves able to teach others, that the deposit of truth may pass securely from hand to hand. The verse displays the transmission of the trust, requiring the endurance of a soldier (v.3).
3:
"Thou therefore endure hardness, as a good soldier of Jesus Christ." The brief verse notes the call to endure hardness as a good soldier of Christ. The grammatical voices note the military image. Barnes and Robertson note the first of three figures (soldier, athlete, farmer) Paul uses for the minister: "thou therefore endure hardness (synkakopatheson — suffer hardship, endure affliction together with me) as a good soldier of Jesus Christ" — Timothy is to bear his sufferings like a soldier on campaign.

On "endure hardness," the witnesses note the soldierly virtue. Barnes and Calvin note that the soldier expects and endures hardship — toil, danger, privation — without complaint; the minister of Christ, enlisted in his service, must likewise be ready to suffer for the cause.

On "a good soldier of Jesus Christ," the grammatical-historical note the warfare: the minister is "a good soldier of Jesus Christ" — enlisted under Christ as his captain, engaged in a spiritual warfare; a good soldier is loyal, brave, and enduring. The verse displays the call to endure hardness as a good soldier.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the first figure — "endure hardness (suffer hardship) as a good soldier of Jesus Christ," bearing sufferings like a soldier on campaign (Barnes, Robertson); the soldier expecting and enduring hardship without complaint, the minister ready to suffer (Barnes, Calvin); "a good soldier of Jesus Christ," enlisted under Christ in spiritual warfare, loyal and brave (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the soldier. The verse marks the call: endure hardness. The verse displays the figure: a good soldier. The verse displays the captain: Jesus Christ. The verse displays the virtue: enduring hardship. The call displays Timothy suffering like a faithful soldier. The verse displays the hardness: endured. The verse displays the soldier: good. The verse displays the warfare: Christ's. The call to endure hardness as a good soldier of Christ displays the first of Paul's images for the minister: Timothy is to bear his sufferings without complaint, like a good soldier on campaign — enlisted under Christ his captain, ready to endure hardship for the cause. The verse displays the soldier's endurance, illustrated by the soldier's single-mindedness (v.4).
4:
"No man that warreth entangleth himself with the affairs of this life; that he may please him who hath chosen him to be a soldier." The brief verse notes the soldier's single-minded freedom from worldly entanglement. The grammatical voices note the image. Barnes and Robertson note the point of the soldier figure: "no man that warreth (serves as a soldier on active duty) entangleth himself with the affairs (the business, pursuits) of this life" — the soldier on campaign sets aside ordinary civilian occupations to give himself wholly to his service.

On "entangleth himself with the affairs of this life," the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Calvin note the lesson for the minister: like the soldier, the minister of Christ is not to be entangled in worldly business and cares that would distract him from his calling; undivided devotion is required.

On "that he may please him who hath chosen him to be a soldier," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the aim: the soldier's freedom from entanglement is "that he may please him who hath chosen (enlisted) him to be a soldier" — the soldier's one concern is to please his commander; so the minister's one aim is to please Christ who enlisted him. The verse displays the soldier's single-minded freedom from worldly entanglement.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure — "no man that warreth (on active duty) entangleth himself with the affairs (business) of this life," the soldier setting aside civilian pursuits for his service (Barnes, Robertson); the minister likewise not entangled in worldly business that distracts, undivided devotion required (Barnes, Calvin); "that he may please him who hath chosen (enlisted) him," the one aim to please his commander, Christ (Wesley). The verse displays single-minded devotion. The verse marks the soldier: not entangled. The verse displays the affairs: of this life. The verse displays the application: undivided devotion. The verse displays the aim: please his commander. The figure displays the minister free from worldly entanglement to please Christ. The verse displays the warring: single-minded. The verse displays the entanglement: avoided. The verse displays the pleasing: of Christ. The soldier's single-minded freedom from worldly entanglement displays the devotion the minister owes: as the soldier on campaign does not entangle himself in civilian affairs but gives himself wholly to please his commander, so the minister, enlisted by Christ, is to be free of distracting worldly cares, that he may please his Lord. The verse displays the soldier's devotion, joined to the athlete's lawful striving (v.5).
5:
"And if a man also strive for masteries, yet is he not crowned, except he strive lawfully." The brief verse notes the athlete crowned only if he strives lawfully. The grammatical voices note the second figure. Barnes and Robertson note the athletic image: "if a man also strive for masteries (athle — competes as an athlete in the games), yet is he not crowned (does not receive the victor's wreath), except he strive (compete) lawfully" — the athlete wins the crown only by competing according to the rules.

On "strive lawfully," the witnesses note the rules. Barnes and Calvin note the meaning: "strive lawfully" — according to the laws of the contest; the runner must keep the course, the wrestler the rules; one who breaks the rules, however he exerts himself, forfeits the prize.

On the application, the grammatical-historical note the lesson: as the athlete is crowned only by lawful striving, so the minister must serve according to God's rules — faithfulness in the appointed way, not mere effort, wins the reward; zeal not regulated by truth and obedience gains no crown. The verse displays the athlete crowned only if he strives lawfully.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the athletic image — "if a man strive for masteries (competes in the games), yet is he not crowned, except he strive lawfully," the athlete winning only by the rules (Barnes, Robertson); "strive lawfully" meaning according to the laws of the contest, rule-breaking forfeiting the prize (Barnes, Calvin); the minister likewise serving by God's rules, faithfulness in the appointed way winning the reward (grammatical-historical). The verse displays lawful striving. The verse marks the athlete: striving. The verse displays the crown: withheld. The verse displays the condition: strive lawfully. The verse displays the application: serve by God's rules. The figure displays the reward won only by lawful service. The verse displays the striving: for masteries. The verse displays the crown: conditional. The verse displays the lawfulness: required. The athlete crowned only if he strives lawfully displays the second of Paul's images: as the competitor wins the victor's wreath only by keeping the rules of the contest, so the minister gains his reward only by serving faithfully according to God's appointed way — zeal alone, unregulated, wins no crown. The verse displays the athlete's lawful striving, joined to the farmer's labour (v.6).
6:
"The husbandman that laboureth must be first partaker of the fruits." The brief verse notes the labouring farmer first to partake of the fruits. The grammatical voices note the third figure. Barnes and Robertson note the agricultural image: "the husbandman (farmer) that laboureth (toils, works hard) must be first partaker of the fruits" — the farmer who works the hardest has the first and rightful claim to the harvest; labour precedes and earns the enjoyment of the fruit.

On "must be first partaker of the fruits," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Ellicott note the application: as the toiling farmer rightly shares first in the crop, so the minister who labours faithfully will partake of the fruits of his labour — whether the present comfort of seeing fruit, or the future reward; toil comes before reward.

On the three figures together, the grammatical-historical note the lesson: soldier, athlete, and farmer all teach that reward follows endurance, discipline, and toil; the minister must labour and endure before he enjoys the fruit. The verse displays the labouring farmer first to partake of the fruits.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the agricultural image — "the husbandman (farmer) that laboureth (toils) must be first partaker of the fruits," the hard-working farmer with first claim to the harvest (Barnes, Robertson); the minister who labours faithfully partaking of the fruits of his labour, toil before reward (Barnes, Ellicott); the three figures together teaching reward follows endurance and toil (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the farmer's reward. The verse marks the farmer: labouring. The verse displays the claim: first partaker. The verse displays the fruit: of his labour. The verse displays the lesson: toil before reward. The figure displays the labourer rewarded with the fruit. The verse displays the husbandman: toiling. The verse displays the partaking: first. The verse displays the fruits: earned. The labouring farmer first to partake of the fruits displays the third of Paul's images: as the hard-working farmer has the first claim to the harvest he has toiled for, so the minister who labours faithfully will share in the fruits of his labour — for toil precedes reward. The verse displays the farmer's reward, to be pondered with the Lord's help (v.7).
7:
"Consider what I say; and the Lord give thee understanding in all things." The brief verse notes the call to consider Paul's words, with God giving understanding. The grammatical voices note the charge to reflect. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's exhortation following the three figures: "consider (noei — think over, ponder, attend to) what I say" — Timothy is to reflect carefully on the lessons of the soldier, athlete, and farmer, to grasp their full meaning.

On "the Lord give thee understanding in all things," the witnesses note the dependence. Barnes and Calvin note the joined prayer: "and the Lord give (grant) thee understanding (synesis — insight, comprehension) in all things" — even reflection needs divine help; Paul prays that the Lord would give Timothy understanding, for true insight is God's gift, not the fruit of human thought alone.

On the balance, the grammatical-historical note the union of effort and grace: Timothy is both to "consider" (to use his own reflection) and to look to the Lord for "understanding" — human diligence and divine illumination together; we ponder, but God gives the insight. The verse displays the call to consider with God giving understanding.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's exhortation — "consider (ponder, attend to) what I say," reflecting on the three figures to grasp their meaning (Barnes, Robertson); "the Lord give thee understanding in all things," reflection needing divine help, insight God's gift (Barnes, Calvin); the union of human diligence and divine illumination, we ponder and God gives insight (grammatical-historical). The verse displays consider and understand. The verse marks the charge: consider. The verse displays the object: what I say. The verse displays the prayer: the Lord give understanding. The verse displays the balance: effort and grace. The call displays Timothy pondering as God gives insight. The verse displays the considering: of his words. The verse displays the understanding: from God. The verse displays the union: diligence and grace. The call to consider Paul's words, with God giving understanding, displays the union of human and divine in grasping the truth: Timothy is to ponder carefully the lessons of soldier, athlete, and farmer — yet Paul prays that the Lord himself would give him understanding in all things, for insight is God's gift. The verse displays the prayer for understanding, before the heart of the gospel (v.8).
8:
"Remember that Jesus Christ of the seed of David was raised from the dead according to my gospel:" The witnesses note the charge to remember the risen Christ of David's seed, the heart of the gospel. On "remember that Jesus Christ... was raised from the dead," the grammatical voices note the central fact. Barnes and Robertson note Paul presses the gospel's core: "remember (keep in mind, hold fast) that Jesus Christ... was raised from the dead" — the resurrection is the heart and pledge of the gospel; Timothy is to keep it ever before him as the ground of his hope and endurance.

On "of the seed of David," the witnesses note the humanity and messiahship. Barnes and Calvin note the phrase: "of the seed of David" — Christ's true humanity and his descent from David, fulfilling the messianic promise; the risen Lord is the true man and the promised Messiah, both natures held together.

On "according to my gospel," the eastern Cyril notes the message: this is "according to my gospel (the gospel I preach)" — the risen, Davidic Christ is the sum of the gospel Paul proclaimed; Timothy's preaching and endurance rest on this. The verse displays the charge to remember the risen Christ of David's seed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the central fact — "remember (hold fast) that Jesus Christ... was raised from the dead," the resurrection the heart and pledge of the gospel, the ground of hope (Barnes, Robertson); "of the seed of David," Christ's true humanity and Davidic messiahship, the promised Messiah (Barnes, Calvin); "according to my gospel," the risen Davidic Christ the sum of Paul's gospel (Cyril). The verse displays the gospel's core. The verse marks the charge: remember. The verse displays the fact: raised from the dead. The verse displays the descent: of David's seed. The verse displays the message: my gospel. The charge displays Timothy holding fast the risen Christ. The verse displays the remembering: of Christ. The verse displays the resurrection: the pledge. The verse displays the gospel: Paul's. The charge to remember the risen Christ of David's seed displays the heart of the gospel Paul sets before Timothy: he is to keep ever in mind Jesus Christ — of the seed of David, the true man and promised Messiah — raised from the dead, the central fact of the gospel and the ground of all endurance. The verse displays the risen Lord, for whom Paul suffers bonds (v.9).
9:
"Wherein I suffer trouble, as an evil doer, even unto bonds; but the word of God is not bound." The witnesses note Paul bound for the gospel, yet the word of God is not bound. On "I suffer trouble, as an evil doer, even unto bonds," the grammatical voices note the suffering. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's testimony: "wherein (in which gospel) I suffer trouble (hardship, affliction), as an evil doer (as if I were a criminal), even unto bonds (chains, imprisonment)" — for preaching this gospel Paul is treated as a malefactor and chained like a common criminal.

On "as an evil doer," the witnesses note the indignity. Barnes and Calvin note the injustice: Paul, who had done only good, was treated "as an evil doer" — the indignity of being chained as a criminal for the sake of the gospel; yet he bore it for Christ.

On "but the word of God is not bound," the eastern Chrysostom notes the triumph: "but the word of God is not bound (not chained, not imprisoned)" — though the preacher is in chains, the gospel runs free; no chains can imprison the truth, which continues to spread and conquer. The verse displays Paul bound for the gospel, yet the word of God is not bound.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's testimony — "I suffer trouble (hardship), as an evil doer (a criminal), even unto bonds," chained like a malefactor for the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); the injustice of being treated "as an evil doer" though he did only good, borne for Christ (Barnes, Calvin); "but the word of God is not bound," the gospel running free though the preacher is chained (Chrysostom). The verse displays the unbound word. The verse marks the suffering: even unto bonds. The verse displays the indignity: as an evil doer. The verse displays the triumph: the word not bound. The verse displays the freedom: of the gospel. The testimony displays Paul chained yet the gospel free. The verse displays the bonds: Paul's. The verse displays the evil doer: so treated. The verse displays the word: unbound. Paul bound for the gospel, yet the word of God is not bound, displays the triumph of the truth over the chains of its preacher: though Paul suffers as a criminal, even to imprisonment, for the gospel's sake, the word of God itself cannot be chained but runs free and conquers. The verse displays the unbound word, for which Paul endures all things (v.10).
10:
"Therefore I endure all things for the elect's sakes, that they may also obtain the salvation which is in Christ Jesus with eternal glory." The witnesses note Paul enduring all things for the elect's salvation and glory. On "I endure all things for the elect's sakes," the grammatical voices note the motive. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's purpose in suffering: "I endure all things for the elect's sakes (for the sake of the chosen, God's people)" — his sufferings are not for himself but for the good of those whom God has chosen; he bears all that they may be saved.

On "the elect," the Reformed note the chosen. Barnes and Calvin note that "the elect" are those God has chosen to salvation; Paul's labours and sufferings serve the gathering and salvation of God's elect, whom the gospel reaches and saves.

On "that they may also obtain the salvation which is in Christ Jesus with eternal glory," the grammatical-historical note the goal: he endures "that they may also obtain (gain, attain) the salvation which is in Christ Jesus with eternal glory" — the end is their salvation, crowned with eternal glory; Paul's sufferings further the elect's attainment of salvation and the glory that follows. The verse displays Paul enduring all things for the elect's salvation and glory.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's motive — "I endure all things for the elect's sakes (the chosen)," his sufferings for the good of God's people not himself (Barnes, Robertson); "the elect," those God chose to salvation, served by Paul's labours (Barnes, Calvin); "that they may also obtain the salvation which is in Christ Jesus with eternal glory," the end their salvation crowned with eternal glory (grammatical-historical). The verse displays endurance for the elect. The verse marks the endurance: all things. The verse displays the motive: the elect's sakes. The verse displays the goal: salvation in Christ. The verse displays the crown: eternal glory. The endurance displays Paul suffering for the elect's salvation. The verse displays the enduring: for them. The verse displays the elect: chosen. The verse displays the glory: eternal. Paul enduring all things for the elect's salvation and glory displays the selfless aim of his sufferings: he bears all his afflictions not for himself but for the sake of God's elect — that they too may obtain the salvation which is in Christ Jesus, crowned with eternal glory. The verse displays the apostle's endurance, sealed by a faithful saying (vv.11-13).
11:
"It is a faithful saying: For if we be dead with him, we shall also live with him:" The witnesses note the faithful saying: dying with Christ, we shall live with him. On "it is a faithful saying," the grammatical voices note the formula. Barnes and Robertson note the trustworthy-word formula (as 1 Timothy 1:15; 3:1): "it is a faithful saying (a trustworthy word)" — introducing a rhythmic, perhaps hymnic or creedal saying that Paul affirms as sure; what follows may be a fragment of an early Christian hymn.

On "if we be dead with him, we shall also live with him," the witnesses note the first clause. Barnes and Calvin note the first of the paired sayings: "if we be dead with him (died with him, in union with his death, whether dying to sin or dying for him in martyrdom), we shall also live with him" — death with Christ leads to life with him; union with his death secures union with his life.

On the meaning, the eastern Chrysostom notes the comfort: the dying believer, sharing Christ's death (in baptism, in mortification, in suffering), is assured of sharing his risen life; the saying steadies the suffering minister with the certainty of life beyond death. The verse displays the faithful saying: dying with Christ, we shall live with him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the formula — "it is a faithful saying (a trustworthy word)," introducing a rhythmic, perhaps hymnic saying Paul affirms (Barnes, Robertson); "if we be dead with him (died with him in union with his death), we shall also live with him," death with Christ leading to life with him (Barnes, Calvin); the dying believer, sharing Christ's death, assured of his risen life (Chrysostom). The verse displays the faithful saying. The verse marks the formula: faithful saying. The verse displays the first clause: dead with him. The verse displays the promise: live with him. The verse displays the comfort: life beyond death. The saying displays union with Christ's death securing his life. The verse displays the faithful: word. The verse displays the dying: with him. The verse displays the living: with him. The faithful saying — dying with Christ, we shall live with him — displays the trustworthy word that steadies the suffering minister: those who die with Christ, sharing his death, will also live with him, sharing his risen life; union with his death secures union with his life. The verse displays the first clause of the saying, joined to suffering and reigning (v.12).
12:
"If we suffer, we shall also reign with him: if we deny him, he also will deny us:" The witnesses note the promise to those who suffer and the warning to those who deny. On "if we suffer, we shall also reign with him," the grammatical voices note the second clause. Barnes and Robertson note the next pair of the faithful saying: "if we suffer (endure, hold out under affliction), we shall also reign with him" — enduring suffering with Christ leads to reigning with him; present suffering is the path to future glory and royal reign.

On "if we deny him, he also will deny us," the witnesses note the solemn warning. Barnes and Calvin note the dark counterpart: "if we deny (disown, renounce) him, he also will deny us" — those who disown Christ (as Peter feared, or the apostate) will be disowned by him (cf. Matthew 10:33); the warning balances the promise, deterring from apostasy under persecution.

On the pairing, the grammatical-historical note the structure: the saying sets reward and penalty side by side — suffer and reign, deny and be denied; the suffering minister is encouraged by the one and warned by the other. The verse displays the promise to those who suffer and the warning to those who deny.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second clause — "if we suffer (endure), we shall also reign with him," enduring suffering leading to reigning, the path to glory (Barnes, Robertson); "if we deny (disown) him, he also will deny us," the apostate disowned by Christ (Matthew 10:33), the warning balancing the promise (Barnes, Calvin); the saying setting reward and penalty side by side (grammatical-historical). The verse displays suffer and deny. The verse marks the promise: suffer and reign. The verse displays the path: suffering to glory. The verse displays the warning: deny and be denied. The verse displays the balance: reward and penalty. The saying displays the encouragement and warning of the suffering minister. The verse displays the suffering: rewarded. The verse displays the reigning: with him. The verse displays the denial: punished. The promise to those who suffer and the warning to those who deny display the two sides of the faithful saying: those who endure suffering with Christ will reign with him, but those who deny him will be denied by him — the one a spur to endurance, the other a warning against apostasy. The verse displays the suffer-and-deny clauses, balanced by Christ's faithfulness (v.13).
13:
"If we believe not, yet he abideth faithful: he cannot deny himself." The witnesses note that Christ abides faithful even when we are faithless. On "if we believe not, yet he abideth faithful," the grammatical voices note the final clause. Barnes and Robertson note the close of the faithful saying: "if we believe not (are faithless, unfaithful), yet he abideth faithful (remains true, trustworthy)" — even human unfaithfulness cannot make Christ unfaithful; his faithfulness does not depend on ours.

On the meaning, interpreters note the double edge. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ's abiding faithfulness cuts both ways: he is faithful to his promises (to save the believing) and faithful to his warnings (to judge the denier); his faithfulness is to his own word and character, not a guarantee that the faithless escape. Some stress the comfort (he keeps his promises despite our weakness); others the solemnity (he will be true to his threatenings).

On "he cannot deny himself," the grammatical-historical note the ground: "he cannot deny (be false to) himself" — Christ's faithfulness flows from his unchangeable nature; he cannot act against his own character and word; were he to be unfaithful, he would deny himself, which is impossible. The verse displays Christ abiding faithful even when we are faithless.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the close — "if we believe not (are faithless), yet he abideth faithful (remains true)," human unfaithfulness cannot make Christ unfaithful (Barnes, Robertson); his faithfulness cutting both ways — true to his promises and to his warnings, faithful to his own word and character (Barnes, Calvin); "he cannot deny himself," his faithfulness flowing from his unchangeable nature, unable to act against himself (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Christ's faithfulness. The verse marks the faithlessness: ours. The verse displays the faithfulness: his abiding. The verse displays the double edge: promise and warning. The verse displays the ground: he cannot deny himself. The saying displays Christ true to his own nature and word. The verse displays the believing not: ours. The verse displays the faithful: he abides. The verse displays the self: not denied. That Christ abides faithful even when we are faithless displays the unchangeable ground of the faithful saying: human unfaithfulness cannot alter Christ's faithfulness — he remains true to his promises and his warnings alike, for he cannot deny himself or act against his own nature. The verse displays Christ's abiding faithfulness, closing the saying and turning to the charge against word-strife (v.14).
14:
"Of these things put them in remembrance, charging them before the Lord that they strive not about words to no profit, but to the subverting of the hearers." The witnesses note the charge against unprofitable word-strife that subverts hearers. On "of these things put them in remembrance," the grammatical voices note the duty. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge to Timothy's teaching: "of these things (the great truths just stated) put them in remembrance (remind the people)" — Timothy is to keep these saving truths before the church, reminding them continually.

On "charging them before the Lord that they strive not about words," the witnesses note the solemn prohibition. Barnes and Calvin note the charge: "charging (solemnly testifying) them before the Lord (in God's sight) that they strive not about words (logomachein — wage word-battles, dispute about mere words)" — they are to be solemnly warned against the empty verbal contentions of the false teachers.

On "to no profit, but to the subverting of the hearers," the puritan Henry notes the harm: such word-strife is "to no profit (useless, unprofitable), but to the subverting (overturning, ruin) of the hearers" — it does no good and positive harm, overthrowing the faith of those who listen. The verse displays the charge against unprofitable word-strife.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge — "of these things put them in remembrance," keeping the saving truths before the church (Barnes, Robertson); "charging them before the Lord that they strive not about words (wage word-battles)," solemnly warning against empty verbal contentions (Barnes, Calvin); "to no profit, but to the subverting (ruin) of the hearers," useless and positively harmful, overthrowing faith (Henry). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the duty: put in remembrance. The verse displays the warning: strive not about words. The verse displays the uselessness: no profit. The verse displays the harm: subverting hearers. The charge displays Timothy warning against ruinous word-strife. The verse displays the remembrance: of truth. The verse displays the strife: forbidden. The verse displays the subverting: of hearers. The charge against unprofitable word-strife that subverts hearers displays Paul's care for sound teaching: Timothy is to keep the saving truths before the people and solemnly warn them, in God's sight, against the empty word-battles of the false teachers — disputes that profit nothing and ruin those who hear them. The verse displays the warning against word-strife, answered by the call to diligent study (v.15).
15:
"Study to shew thyself approved unto God, a workman that needeth not to be ashamed, rightly dividing the word of truth." The witnesses note the charge to be an approved workman rightly dividing the word. On "study to shew thyself approved unto God," the grammatical voices note the diligence. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's positive charge over against word-strife: "study (spoudason — be diligent, give diligence, make every effort) to shew (present) thyself approved (dokimon — tested and approved, as metal proved genuine) unto God" — Timothy is to labour to be approved by God, not by men; God's approval is the aim.

On "a workman that needeth not to be ashamed," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Calvin note the image: "a workman (labourer) that needeth not to be ashamed" — like a skilled craftsman whose work bears inspection; the faithful minister, doing his work well, has no cause for shame when his labour is examined.

On "rightly dividing the word of truth," the grammatical-historical note the central task: "rightly dividing (orthotomeo — cutting straight, handling rightly, as cutting a straight path or rightly portioning) the word of truth" — handling Scripture correctly, dividing it aright, giving each hearer his due portion; the metaphor of cutting a straight road or rightly carving. The verse displays the charge to be an approved workman rightly dividing the word.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's positive charge — "study (be diligent, make every effort) to shew thyself approved (tested and genuine) unto God," labouring for God's approval not men's (Barnes, Robertson); "a workman that needeth not to be ashamed," like a skilled craftsman whose work bears inspection (Barnes, Calvin); "rightly dividing (cutting straight, handling rightly) the word of truth," handling Scripture correctly, giving each his portion (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the approved workman. The verse marks the diligence: study. The verse displays the approval: unto God. The verse displays the figure: an unashamed workman. The verse displays the task: rightly dividing the word. The charge displays Timothy a faithful, skilled handler of the truth. The verse displays the studying: diligent. The verse displays the workman: approved. The verse displays the dividing: rightly. The charge to be an approved workman rightly dividing the word displays the heart of faithful ministry: Timothy is to give diligence to be approved by God — a workman with no cause for shame — handling the word of truth correctly, cutting it straight and giving each hearer his due. The verse displays the diligent workman, over against profane babblings (v.16).
16:
"But shun profane and vain babblings: for they will increase unto more ungodliness." The brief verse notes the charge to shun profane babblings that breed ungodliness. The grammatical voices note the prohibition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's command over against rightly dividing the word: "shun (periistaso — avoid, turn away from, give a wide berth to) profane (unholy, irreligious) and vain (empty, idle) babblings (kenophonias — empty chatter, foolish talk)" — Timothy is to keep clear of the empty, irreligious talk of the false teachers.

On "profane and vain babblings," the witnesses note their character. Barnes and Calvin note that these "babblings" were the empty speculations and disputes of the errorists — irreligious in tendency, idle and unprofitable in substance; mere noise without edification.

On "they will increase unto more ungodliness," the grammatical-historical note the danger: such babblings "will increase (advance, make progress) unto more ungodliness (impiety)" — they do not stay harmless but grow worse, leading on to greater impiety; error is progressive, spreading and deepening ungodliness. The verse displays the charge to shun profane babblings.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's command — "shun (avoid, give a wide berth to) profane (unholy) and vain (empty) babblings (empty chatter)," keeping clear of the false teachers' empty talk (Barnes, Robertson); the babblings the empty speculations of the errorists, irreligious and unprofitable (Barnes, Calvin); "they will increase unto more ungodliness," not staying harmless but growing worse, error progressive (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the command: shun babblings. The verse displays the character: profane and vain. The verse displays the danger: increase ungodliness. The verse displays the progression: error spreading. The charge displays Timothy avoiding ungodly babbling. The verse displays the babblings: shunned. The verse displays the profanity: empty. The verse displays the ungodliness: increasing. The charge to shun profane babblings that breed ungodliness displays Paul's guarding of the truth: Timothy is to give a wide berth to the empty, irreligious talk of the false teachers — for such babblings do not stay harmless but grow worse, advancing ever further into ungodliness. The verse displays the warning against error's spread, likened to a canker (v.17).
17:
"And their word will eat as doth a canker: of whom is Hymenaeus and Philetus;" The brief verse notes the spreading corruption of error, like a canker. The grammatical voices note the figure of disease. Barnes and Robertson note the vivid image: "their word (the teaching of the errorists) will eat (have pasture, spread, consume) as doth a canker (gangraina — gangrene, a spreading, eating sore)" — false teaching spreads like gangrene, eating away the healthy flesh, consuming all it touches.

On "as doth a canker," the witnesses note the malignancy. Barnes and Calvin note the force of the figure: gangrene spreads relentlessly, destroying living tissue and proving fatal if not cut off; so error, if unchecked, spreads through the church, corrupting and destroying; it must be stopped, not tolerated.

On "Hymenaeus and Philetus," the grammatical-historical note the named errorists: "of whom is Hymenaeus and Philetus" — two named teachers of this error (Hymenaeus also in 1 Timothy 1:20), singled out as concrete examples of those whose corrupting doctrine spread like gangrene. The verse displays the spreading corruption of error, like a canker.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vivid image — "their word (the errorists' teaching) will eat (spread, consume) as doth a canker (gangrene)," false teaching spreading like gangrene, eating healthy flesh (Barnes, Robertson); gangrene spreading relentlessly and fatally if not cut off, so error corrupting the church if unchecked (Barnes, Calvin); "Hymenaeus and Philetus," two named errorists, concrete examples (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the spreading canker. The verse marks the word: of the errorists. The verse displays the figure: a canker. The verse displays the malignancy: spreading, fatal. The verse displays the named: Hymenaeus and Philetus. The corruption displays error eating through the church like gangrene. The verse displays the eating: spreading. The verse displays the canker: malignant. The verse displays the names: two. The spreading corruption of error, like a canker, displays the danger Paul warns against: the word of the false teachers spreads like gangrene, eating away the church's health and proving fatal if unchecked — as in Hymenaeus and Philetus, named examples of this corrupting doctrine. The verse displays the canker of error, whose specific heresy is named (v.18).
18:
"Who concerning the truth have erred, saying that the resurrection is past already; and overthrow the faith of some." The witnesses note the error that the resurrection is past, overthrowing some. On "who concerning the truth have erred," the grammatical voices note the deviation. Barnes and Robertson note the description of the errorists: "who concerning the truth have erred (astochesan — missed the mark, swerved, gone astray)" — Hymenaeus and Philetus had wandered from the truth, missing the target of sound doctrine.

On "saying that the resurrection is past already," the witnesses note the specific heresy. Barnes and Calvin note the error: "saying that the resurrection is past already" — they taught that the resurrection had already occurred, spiritualizing it (taking it as merely the new birth or a moral/spiritual rising, or already accomplished), and so denying the future bodily resurrection of believers; an over-realized, spiritualized eschatology.

On "overthrow the faith of some," the grammatical-historical note the damage: this error "overthrow (anatrepousin — overturn, subvert) the faith of some" — it did real harm, upsetting and destroying the faith of some who embraced it; denying the resurrection cut at the root of Christian hope. The verse displays the error that the resurrection is past, overthrowing some.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deviation — "who concerning the truth have erred (missed the mark, swerved)," wandering from sound doctrine (Barnes, Robertson); "saying that the resurrection is past already," spiritualizing the resurrection as already occurred and denying the future bodily resurrection, an over-realized eschatology (Barnes, Calvin); "overthrow (subvert) the faith of some," upsetting and destroying the faith of those who embraced it (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the error. The verse marks the deviation: erred from the truth. The verse displays the heresy: resurrection past already. The verse displays the nature: spiritualized. The verse displays the damage: faith overthrown. The error displays a denial of the resurrection ruining some. The verse displays the erring: from truth. The verse displays the resurrection: denied future. The verse displays the faith: overthrown. The error that the resurrection is past, overthrowing some, displays the specific heresy of Hymenaeus and Philetus: having swerved from the truth, they taught that the resurrection had already occurred — spiritualizing it and denying the future bodily resurrection — and so overturned the faith of some. The verse displays the resurrection error, against the sure foundation of God (v.19).
19:
"Nevertheless the foundation of God standeth sure, having this seal, The Lord knoweth them that are his. And, Let every one that nameth the name of Christ depart from iniquity." The witnesses note God's sure foundation with its twofold seal. On "the foundation of God standeth sure," the grammatical voices note the firmness. Barnes and Robertson note the comfort against the errorists' damage: "nevertheless the foundation of God standeth sure (stands firm, is unshaken)" — though some be overthrown, God's foundation — whether his church, his elect, his truth, or his eternal purpose — remains firm and immovable.

On "having this seal, The Lord knoweth them that are his," the witnesses note the first inscription. Barnes and Calvin note the figure of a foundation-stone bearing an inscription (a seal): "The Lord knoweth (recognizes, owns) them that are his" — God's side: he infallibly knows and owns his own; the elect are secure, known to God, however error rages; none of his are lost.

On "Let every one that nameth the name of Christ depart from iniquity," the puritan Henry notes the second inscription: the human side: "let every one that nameth the name of Christ (professes to be his) depart from (renounce, forsake) iniquity" — those who claim Christ must prove it by holiness; the seal has two sides — God's knowledge of his own, and the believer's departure from sin. The verse displays God's sure foundation with its twofold seal.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comfort — "the foundation of God standeth sure (firm, unshaken)," God's foundation (church, elect, truth) remaining firm though some fall (Barnes, Robertson); the first inscription "The Lord knoweth them that are his," God's side — he infallibly knows and owns his own, the elect secure (Barnes, Calvin); the second "let every one that nameth Christ depart from iniquity," the human side — professors proving their claim by holiness (Henry). The verse displays the sure foundation. The verse marks the firmness: standeth sure. The verse displays the first seal: the Lord knoweth his own. The verse displays the second: depart from iniquity. The verse displays the two sides: God's and man's. The foundation displays God's people secure and called to holiness. The verse displays the foundation: sure. The verse displays the knowing: God's. The verse displays the departing: from iniquity. God's sure foundation with its twofold seal displays the comfort and the duty amid error: though the faith of some is overthrown, God's foundation stands firm, sealed on God's side by "The Lord knoweth them that are his," and on man's side by "Let every one that nameth Christ depart from iniquity." The verse displays the sure foundation, illustrated by vessels in a great house (vv.20-21).
20:
"But in a great house there are not only vessels of gold and of silver, but also of wood and of earth; and some to honour, and some to dishonour." The witnesses note the great house with vessels of honour and dishonour. On "in a great house there are not only vessels of gold and of silver, but also of wood and of earth," the grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note the illustration of the church (or the visible professing body): "in a great house (a large mansion) there are not only vessels (utensils) of gold and of silver (precious, for noble use), but also of wood and of earth (common, cheap)" — a great household contains vessels of varied worth.

On "some to honour, and some to dishonour," the witnesses note the two uses. Barnes and Chrysostom note the application: "some to honour (for honourable, noble use), and some to dishonour (for base, menial use)" — so in the church there are members of differing character, some honourable (true and useful), some dishonourable (false or unworthy); the mixed state of the visible church.

On the meaning, the grammatical-historical note the lesson: the presence of unworthy vessels (the errorists) need not surprise or shake; even a great house has common and base vessels alongside the precious; so the church contains both. The verse displays the great house with vessels of honour and dishonour.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure — "in a great house there are vessels of gold and silver (precious), but also of wood and earth (common)," a great household holding vessels of varied worth (Barnes, Robertson); "some to honour, and some to dishonour," members of differing character, true and false, the mixed visible church (Barnes, Chrysostom); the unworthy vessels need not shake us, even a great house has base vessels (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the varied vessels. The verse marks the house: great. The verse displays the precious: gold and silver. The verse displays the common: wood and earth. The verse displays the uses: honour and dishonour. The figure displays the church holding worthy and unworthy members. The verse displays the vessels: varied. The verse displays the honour: some. The verse displays the dishonour: some. The great house with vessels of honour and dishonour displays the mixed state of the visible church: as a great household contains vessels both precious and common, for noble and for base use, so the church holds members of differing worth — the presence of the unworthy (the errorists) no cause for surprise. The verse displays the varied vessels, calling each to purge himself for honour (v.21).
21:
"If a man therefore purge himself from these, he shall be a vessel unto honour, sanctified, and meet for the master's use, and prepared unto every good work." The witnesses note the call to purge oneself to be a vessel unto honour. On "if a man therefore purge himself from these," the grammatical voices note the cleansing. Barnes and Robertson note the application of the vessel figure: "if a man purge (ekkathare — cleanse, purify) himself from these (from the dishonourable vessels, the errorists and their corruptions)" — by separating from the false and the impure, a man cleanses himself.

On "he shall be a vessel unto honour, sanctified," the witnesses note the result. Barnes and Calvin note the outcome: he "shall be a vessel unto honour (for noble use), sanctified (set apart, made holy)" — the one who purges himself becomes a vessel of honour, set apart and consecrated for God's service.

On "meet for the master's use, and prepared unto every good work," the grammatical-historical note the usefulness: "meet (useful, fit) for the master's use, and prepared (made ready) unto every good work" — fit for the Master's hand, ready for every good service; the cleansed vessel is useful and prepared. The verse displays the call to purge oneself to be a vessel unto honour.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the application — "if a man purge (cleanse) himself from these (the dishonourable vessels and corruptions)," separating from the false and impure (Barnes, Robertson); "he shall be a vessel unto honour (noble use), sanctified (set apart, holy)," consecrated for God's service (Barnes, Calvin); "meet (fit) for the master's use, and prepared unto every good work," useful and ready for every good service (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the cleansed vessel. The verse marks the cleansing: purge himself. The verse displays the result: a vessel unto honour. The verse displays the consecration: sanctified. The verse displays the usefulness: meet for the master. The call displays the purified believer fit for God's use. The verse displays the purging: from these. The verse displays the honour: a vessel. The verse displays the good work: prepared. The call to purge oneself to be a vessel unto honour displays the believer's responsibility amid the mixed church: by cleansing himself from the corruptions of the false, a man becomes a vessel of honour — sanctified, fit for the Master's use, and prepared for every good work. The verse displays the consecrated vessel, called to flee lusts and follow holiness (v.22).
22:
"Flee also youthful lusts: but follow righteousness, faith, charity, peace, with them that call on the Lord out of a pure heart." The witnesses note the charge to flee youthful lusts and follow righteousness with the pure. On "flee also youthful lusts," the grammatical voices note the negative. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge to the young minister: "flee (pheuge — run from, escape) also youthful lusts (the desires and passions of youth — not only sensual, but pride, ambition, impatience, contentiousness, love of novelty)" — Timothy is to run from the characteristic temptations of his age.

On "but follow righteousness, faith, charity, peace," the witnesses note the positive pursuit. Barnes and Calvin note the things to pursue: "follow (pursue, chase after) righteousness (uprightness), faith, charity (love), peace" — over against fleeing lusts, Timothy is to pursue the cluster of graces; flight from evil joined with pursuit of good.

On "with them that call on the Lord out of a pure heart," the puritan Henry notes the company: this pursuit is "with them that call on the Lord (true believers, worshippers) out of a pure heart" — in the fellowship of sincere believers; the graces are pursued together with the pure-hearted people of God, not in isolation. The verse displays the charge to flee youthful lusts and follow righteousness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the negative — "flee (run from) youthful lusts (the passions of youth — sensual, but also pride, ambition, contentiousness)," running from the temptations of his age (Barnes, Robertson); "follow (pursue) righteousness, faith, charity (love), peace," pursuing the cluster of graces, flight joined with pursuit (Barnes, Calvin); "with them that call on the Lord out of a pure heart," in the fellowship of sincere believers (Henry). The verse displays flee and follow. The verse marks the flight: youthful lusts. The verse displays the pursuit: righteousness, faith, love, peace. The verse displays the company: the pure-hearted. The verse displays the balance: flee evil, follow good. The charge displays Timothy fleeing lust and pursuing grace with the pure. The verse displays the lusts: fled. The verse displays the graces: followed. The verse displays the pure heart: company. The charge to flee youthful lusts and follow righteousness with the pure displays the path of holiness for the young minister: Timothy is to run from the passions of youth and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace — together with all who call on the Lord out of a pure heart. The verse displays the pursuit of grace, over against foolish questions (v.23).
23:
"But foolish and unlearned questions avoid, knowing that they do gender strifes." The brief verse notes the charge to avoid foolish questions that breed strife. The grammatical voices note the prohibition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's command: "foolish (silly, senseless) and unlearned (uninstructed, ignorant, undisciplined) questions (speculations, debates) avoid (paraitou — refuse, decline, have nothing to do with)" — Timothy is to refuse to engage in the silly, ignorant disputes of the false teachers.

On "foolish and unlearned questions," the witnesses note their character. Barnes and Calvin note that these "questions" were the idle speculations and controversies of the errorists — foolish in being pointless, unlearned in springing from ignorance rather than true knowledge; not worth engaging.

On "knowing that they do gender strifes," the grammatical-historical note the reason: such questions "gender (beget, breed, give birth to) strifes (quarrels, contentions)" — they produce nothing but quarrels; the certain fruit of these debates is contention, not edification, and so they are to be avoided. The verse displays the charge to avoid foolish questions that breed strife.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's command — "foolish (senseless) and unlearned (ignorant) questions (speculations) avoid (refuse, decline)," refusing the silly disputes of the errorists (Barnes, Robertson); the questions idle speculations, pointless and springing from ignorance (Barnes, Calvin); "knowing that they do gender (breed) strifes," producing only quarrels not edification (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the command: avoid the questions. The verse displays the character: foolish and unlearned. The verse displays the reason: they breed strife. The verse displays the fruit: quarrels. The charge displays Timothy declining strife-breeding debates. The verse displays the questions: avoided. The verse displays the folly: pointless. The verse displays the strifes: bred. The charge to avoid foolish questions that breed strife displays Paul's wisdom for the minister: Timothy is to refuse to engage the silly, ignorant speculations of the false teachers — knowing that such debates beget nothing but quarrels. The verse displays the avoidance of strife, requiring the servant's gentleness (v.24). What the apostle sets his face against here is not honest inquiry or the patient teaching of those who genuinely seek to learn, but the restless appetite for controversy that marked the false teachers, who delighted in raising endless speculative puzzles for their own sake; such questions, he warns, are foolish in that they lead nowhere and unlearned in that they spring not from any true grasp of the things of God but from an ignorant love of dispute, and their one certain harvest is the breeding of quarrels that tear the peace of the church without ever advancing a single soul toward godliness.
24:
"And the servant of the Lord must not strive; but be gentle unto all men, apt to teach, patient," The witnesses note the servant of the Lord marked by gentleness, not strife. On "the servant of the Lord must not strive," the grammatical voices note the prohibition. Barnes and Robertson note the character of the true minister: "the servant (bondservant) of the Lord must not strive (machesthai — quarrel, wrangle, fight)" — the minister is not to be contentious or quarrelsome; strife ill becomes Christ's servant, whatever the provocation.

On "but be gentle unto all men, apt to teach," the witnesses note the positive graces. Barnes and Calvin note the virtues: "but be gentle (epion — mild, kind) unto all men, apt to teach (didaktikon — skilled and ready to teach)" — over against strife, the minister is to be gentle to all and able to teach; mildness joined with teaching skill.

On "patient," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the third grace: "patient (anexikakon — forbearing, bearing evil without resentment, enduring wrong)" — the minister must bear patiently with the difficult, the opposing, and the ignorant; forbearance under provocation. The verse displays the servant of the Lord marked by gentleness, not strife.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the character — "the servant (bondservant) of the Lord must not strive (quarrel, wrangle)," not contentious whatever the provocation (Barnes, Robertson); "but be gentle (mild, kind) unto all men, apt to teach (skilled, ready to teach)," mildness joined with teaching ability (Barnes, Calvin); "patient (forbearing, bearing evil without resentment)," bearing patiently with the difficult (Wesley). The verse displays the gentle servant. The verse marks the prohibition: must not strive. The verse displays the gentleness: to all men. The verse displays the skill: apt to teach. The verse displays the forbearance: patient. The character displays the minister gentle, teaching, and forbearing. The verse displays the servant: not striving. The verse displays the gentleness: shown. The verse displays the patience: held. The servant of the Lord marked by gentleness, not strife, displays the spirit the minister must have: the Lord's servant is not to wrangle or quarrel, but to be gentle to all, skilled and ready to teach, and patient — forbearing under provocation. The verse displays the gentle servant, instructing opposers in meekness (v.25).
25:
"In meekness instructing those that oppose themselves; if God peradventure will give them repentance to the acknowledging of the truth;" The witnesses note instructing opposers in meekness, that God may grant repentance — and the question of repentance. On "in meekness instructing those that oppose themselves," the grammatical voices note the manner. Barnes and Robertson note the spirit of correction: "in meekness (gentleness) instructing (correcting, schooling) those that oppose themselves (set themselves in opposition to the truth)" — those in error are to be corrected not with anger or denunciation but with patient gentleness, the wisest as well as the kindest course.

On "if God peradventure will give them repentance," interpreters note the gift of repentance. Most — Wesley ("it is wholly his work"), Ellicott, Gill, and the common reading — take it that "God may give (grant) them repentance" — repentance is God's gift, granted by him; the minister instructs, but the change of heart is God's to give. Even Wesley, the Arminian, affirms repentance here is "wholly his work." Barnes, however, dissents on the grammar: he holds that the Greek (mēpote) refers not to God's giving but to the opposers' posture — they set themselves in opposition "lest" they be brought to repentance, the deliberate self-hardening of those who will not repent.

On "to the acknowledging of the truth," the grammatical-historical note the end: the repentance leads "to the acknowledging (full knowledge, recognition) of the truth" — a change of mind that issues in embracing and confessing the truth. The verse displays instructing opposers in meekness, that God may grant repentance.

The witnesses agree the manner: opposers are to be corrected "in meekness (gentleness)," patiently and kindly, that they may be won (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley). And most agree the substance: repentance unto the acknowledging of the truth is God's gift, which the minister hopes God may grant — even Wesley owning it "wholly his work" (Ellicott, Gill, Wesley). But Barnes dissents on the grammar of "if God peradventure," referring mēpote not to God's giving but to the opposers' deliberate self-hardening against repentance. The verse displays the gift of repentance. The verse marks the manner: in meekness. The verse displays the objects: those who oppose. The verse displays the debate: repentance God's gift, or the opposers' posture. The verse displays the end: acknowledging the truth. The instruction displays opposers gently corrected, that God may grant repentance. The verse displays the meekness: shown. The verse displays the repentance: God's gift. The verse displays the truth: acknowledged. Instructing opposers in meekness, that God may grant repentance, displays the gentle hope of the minister and a nuance: those in error are to be corrected with gentleness, in the hope that God may give them repentance unto the truth — repentance owned as God's gift even by Wesley, though Barnes reads "if peradventure" rather of the opposers' self-hardening. The verse displays the hope of repentance, that they escape the devil's snare (v.26).
26:
"And that they may recover themselves out of the snare of the devil, who are taken captive by him at his will." The witnesses note the hope of recovery from the devil's snare. On "that they may recover themselves out of the snare of the devil," the grammatical voices note the deliverance. Barnes and Robertson note the further aim of the gentle instruction: "that they may recover themselves (ananepso — return to soberness, come to their senses, awake as from a drunken stupor) out of the snare (trap, net) of the devil" — the hope is that the opposers may be freed from the trap in which Satan has caught them.

On "the snare of the devil," the witnesses note the captivity. Barnes and Calvin note that error and opposition to the truth are a "snare of the devil" — Satan has trapped these persons in their false views; they are caught, needing deliverance, not merely mistaken.

On "who are taken captive by him at his will," interpreters note the captivity's terms. Barnes and Ellicott note the phrase: "who are taken captive by him (the devil) at his will (to do his will)" — held captive by Satan to serve his purposes; though some refer the pronouns differently (taken captive by the servant of the Lord, to do God's will), most take it as captured by the devil to do the devil's will. The verse displays the hope of recovery from the devil's snare.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the aim — "that they may recover themselves (come to their senses, awake as from a stupor) out of the snare (trap) of the devil," freed from Satan's trap (Barnes, Robertson); error a "snare of the devil," Satan having trapped them, needing deliverance (Barnes, Calvin); "taken captive by him at his will," held by Satan to do his will (most; some refer it to recovery to God's will, Barnes, Ellicott). The verse displays recovery from the snare. The verse marks the deliverance: recover themselves. The verse displays the trap: the snare of the devil. The verse displays the captivity: taken captive. The verse displays the hope: freed by repentance. The aim displays the opposers delivered from Satan's snare. The verse displays the recovering: awakening. The verse displays the snare: the devil's. The verse displays the captivity: broken. The hope of recovery from the devil's snare displays the goal of the minister's gentle correction: that those held captive in error by the devil might, through repentance, come to their senses and be freed from the snare in which Satan has trapped them. The verse displays the deliverance hoped for, closing the chapter on the faithful servant of the Lord.
